title: Module 1 Quiz
description: Test your understanding of the two-brain system, arousal, breathing, and attention.

blocks.content: Seven questions covering the core concepts from Module 1. No tricks — this is retrieval practice, which is one of the most effective ways to lock knowledge into long-term memory.

blocks.question: 1. What does "the gap" refer to in the two-brain model?
blocks.explanation: Correct. The gap is the trainable space between the chimp's reactive impulse and your deliberate response. Widening it is the foundation of the entire course.

options.text: The gap between your fitness and your competitors' fitness
The space between stimulus and response — where the chimp fires and the coach can intervene
The gap between your training load and your recovery
The distance to the rider ahead of you

blocks.question: 2. The sprint finish paradox — runners speeding up in the final miles despite apparent exhaustion — supports which theory?
blocks.explanation: Correct. Noakes argued that if fatigue were purely peripheral, the sprint finish shouldn't be possible. The energy was there the whole time — the brain was holding it in reserve.

options.text: The peripheral fatigue model (muscles hit a physical wall)
The Central Governor Theory (the brain was holding reserve capacity)

blocks.question: 3. Which of the following shifts arousal upward (toward the sympathetic/fight-or-flight side)?
blocks.explanation: Correct. Race starts, crowds, competition, and perceived threats all activate the sympathetic nervous system, pushing arousal higher on the dial.

options.text: Race start adrenaline, crowd energy, and competitive pressure
Extending the exhale longer than the inhale

blocks.question: 4. The 6-2-7 protocol: Inhale for ___, hold for ___, exhale for ___.
blocks.explanation: Correct. Inhale 6, hold 2, exhale 7. The exhale is longer than the inhale because expiration activates the parasympathetic brake.

blocks.question: 5. An athlete scanning the race field, reading terrain ahead, and assessing competitor positions is using which attention mode?
blocks.explanation: Correct. Broad-External is tactical awareness — scanning the environment, reading terrain, assessing the race situation.

blocks.question: 6. In-the-moment RPE is typically ___ compared to retrospective RPE rated 10 minutes later.
blocks.explanation: Correct. The chimp layers fear, frustration, and catastrophic thinking on top of the raw physical sensation. A 7 becomes a 9. That gap is your brain's tax.

options.text: Lower — effort feels easier in the moment
Higher — the chimp adds an emotional markup of 1-3 points

blocks.question: 7. Your RPE feels like a 9 mid-race. Based on the RPE Journal concept, the actual physical effort is probably a:
blocks.explanation: Correct. "The chimp says 9. It's probably a 7. I can do a 7." That reframe — grounded in your own RPE Journal data — is the practical application of this lesson.

options.text: 10 — it's actually worse than it feels
7 — the chimp is adding a 1-3 point surcharge
6 — the chimp typically adds a 3-4 point markup
